name: Data Path
description: A study of the microarchitecture, the data path, internal registers, the ALU, buses, and the timing of the execution subcycles.

questions.question: What is the main composition of the Data Path in the described microarchitecture?
options: Only the main memory and the integrated disk and video controllers.
The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit), a shifter, and 32-bit internal registers (such as PC, SP, and MDR).
The control store (ROM) and the MPC register.
L1, L2, and L3 caches and the external system buses on the motherboard.

questions.question: In the example machine's Data Path, which structure provides the right input (input B) to the ALU?
options: The H register.
The C bus.
The B bus, which can be loaded from one of nine different sources.
Main memory directly through the MAR register.

questions.question: Which register is connected exclusively to the left input (input A) of the ALU in the data path?
options: The H register.
The PC register.
The TOS register.
The MBR register.

questions.question: Is it possible to read from and write to the same register within a single clock cycle in this data path?
options: No, attempting to read and write in the same cycle produces electrical garbage due to a short circuit.
Yes, because the read onto the B bus happens at the start of the cycle and the write from the C bus happens near the end of the cycle.
Yes, because the processor has multiple independent data paths (superscalar architecture).
No, the IJVM virtual machine strictly forbids reading and writing 32-bit registers simultaneously.

questions.question: How can the time of a data path cycle be implicitly divided, and when does it start?
options: It starts on the rising edge of the clock and is divided into 2 subcycles (fetch and execute).
It starts on the falling edge of the clock and is divided into 4 subcycles: signal settling, loading the B bus, ALU/shifter operation, and propagation on the C bus.
It starts with the read of ROM memory and has no subcycles, being one continuous and uninterrupted flow.
It starts with the operating system interrupt and is divided into micro, nano, and pico cycles.

questions.question: At what exact moment are the results processed and propagated through the C bus actually stored (written) into the destination registers?
options: At the exact start of the pulse, right on the falling edge of the clock.
Immediately after the ALU finishes the calculation, regardless of the clock oscillation.
Near the end of the cycle, triggered by the rising edge of the clock pulse.
Only on the next clock cycle, after the read confirmation of the MIR register.

questions.question: What is the function of the control line that acts on the shifter right after the ALU in the data path?
options: It converts the 32-bit data to 8 bits so it fits into the MBR.
It allows shifting the ALU output left (SLL8) by 1 byte or right (SRA1) by 1 bit.
It mandatorily performs the mathematical operation of division by 3.
It halts execution of the microprogram if the ALU encounters an invalid opcode.

questions.question: To access memory, the machine has two different ports. Which registers control the 32-bit, word-addressable port?
options: PC and MBR.
LV and SP.
MAR and MDR.
CPP and TOS.

questions.question: The 8-bit (byte-addressable) memory port has a restriction and a main purpose in the data path. What are they?
options: It can read and write generic user data to the hard disk.
It can only read data from memory and is used mainly by the PC register to fetch the executable program (sequence of bytes) into the MBR.
It writes local variables directly onto the stack to facilitate recursion.
It transfers data exclusively between the C bus and the H register, bypassing the ALU.

questions.question: At the interface of the 8-bit register (MBR) with the 32-bit B bus, there are two conversion options supported by the data path. What are they?
options: Multiplication by 4 (for addresses) or division by 4.
Logical bit inversion (NOT) or a continuous circular left shift.
Sign extension (copying the leftmost bit) or zero-filling the upper 24 bits (unsigned value).
Direct storage in the H register or direct reverse sending to the C bus.

questions.question: What does a 'light (white) arrow' located beneath a register indicate in the schematic diagram of the data path?
options: That the register is being overwritten with new data coming from the C bus.
That the register is connected autonomously and directly to main memory.
A control signal that enables that register's output to supply data to the B bus.
An exclusive falling-edge clock signal to reset that electrical component.

questions.question: To ensure that operations on the data path do not cause physical or logical problems (electrical garbage), what fundamental rule is applied to the B bus?
options: The B bus must be reset to 0xFFFFFFFF at each new subcycle.
More than one register must never be enabled at a time as a source to drive the B bus.
The ALU will ignore the B bus data unless it receives a permission signal from main memory.
All registers must be copied to the B bus simultaneously and the ALU will do the filtering.
